item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I see some interesting connections with this and SCP-1009.
Both reference invasive, possibly extra-dimensional, blue-purple plant life; that has some relationship to sound.

I'm imagining that the land of purple ivy is an alternate dimension very close to our own, just with an ever so slightly different quantum vibration frequency.
The distress calls of 1028 jostle the sub-atomic particles of our universe juuuuust right to allow an overlap with that other place. Given a little bit of time, the plant life on the other side does what invasive species tend to do and spreads out into the new territory.
As the purple ivy grows, its cells naturally continue to resonate at its home frequency. Widening the area of dimensional overlap, and making it easier for more ivy to grow wherever ivy has already made the crossing.

Noise of sufficient volume (from the containment of 1009) has the opposite effect of 1028 song. It jostles the particles of the ivy just enough to knock them out of sync with the other plane. Not enough to break the connection completely1, but enough to prevent further incursion.
That's why any loud sound will work to contain 1009. The precise pattern of 1028 is needed to make the alignment, but any vibes will do to disrupt that harmony.

Footnotes
1. For that I suppose you would need to alter the fundamental quantum frequency of the ivy. Which would take a lot more than some speaker stacks!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh and just when I thought Kudzu and Japanese Knotweed teaming up would be a bad thing to control, now we have extradimensional Kudzu. As a budding botanist the thought of a new god-like plant scares me to no end. Now you say the birds can create (directly or indirectly) these multicolored plants? Where else have we seen multi-colored extradimensional plants? (hinthinhintscp-1009hinthint) I wonder if they have anything in common…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I enjoyed the concept, but I was hesitant to upvote this one-until I read the interview transcript, that is. There's something quite eerie about these beautiful, relatively harmless creatures using their abilities to force observers to perceive an abandoned world retaken by nature, specifically nature that they seem capable of controlling. Well done.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
First off, the containment procedures contradict themselves - the final paragraph basically says "You should kill these things if you find them, but if you manage to capture one without killing it, send it over to the containment site."

Second…I want to like this, I really do, but it doesn't have much going for it. It's a flock of multicolored birds…It's somewhat anomalous, yes, but is it really an SCP? I see people in previous comments talking about an alternate universe, but I feel like that's grabbing at straws - the impression I got was that the birds just make you hallucinate stuff for no reason, and occasionally make weird plants grow for no reason. That's another thing, too - there's no reason for the birds to have these effects. What purposes do they serve? I mean, there's the neutralizing predators thing with the hallucinations, but really? It's kind of OP, and the weird plants thing makes no sense at all. I can see people saying in response to this, "It's an SCP, it's not supposed to make sense!" Yes, I know that, but there's not making sense because it's a scientific anomaly, and there's not making sense because it's downright silly and phoned in.

I'm usually very generous with my upvotes, but while there's nothing incredibly wrong with this article, it just…doesn't work for me. No vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Needs more to be interesting. Right now, it's just a trip bird.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I have changed the closing statement.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I've changed the closing statement.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It just feels a little disjointed. Flocks of hallucinatory birds, but are really pulling an alternate universe over to ours. I don't know. I like the interview log, especially if it is not just a hallucination. Cautious upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like the overall concept, but I don't think the interview logs add much of anything. In addition the closing statement, beside being an example of horrible grammar and writing, is not even needed, since it's pretty obvious what amnesiacs are for. "Subject was given Class B amnesiacs and released" is all that is required. I also don't think the plant thing is either interesting or necessary.

I will refrain from downvoting for now, but at the least the closing statement needs to be fixed before I would upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Mostly because they've got hard jobs, and should be rewarded with short-term, non-dehydrating acid trips.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm glad you get what I was trying for. And why do you think the aviary should be open to researchers during their lunch hour? If you have an idea, I'd love to hear it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: WHAT.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Good concept, I like it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like the overall idea, but while I am upvoting I think the closing statement needs to be gone. It's redundant on face, and unnecessary overall. Of course the amnesiacs were so he would forget, and I don't think the Foundation would bother to record what he's going to think instead.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like this a lot.
Just by flocking into a populated area, these things could kill hundreds without even trying. And they have a near extra-dimensional feel to them.
On an unrelated note, I think the Foundation should open that aviary to researchers on their lunch hour.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The tone is really careless here and kept me from enjoying the article.

Please note that this is supposed to be a scientific briefing from an all-powerful shadowy research organization. Use of the passive voice and the overuse of "should" do not fit into that setting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: For some reason, I just really don't like it.The interview is nice, but there isn't really any draw for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: And I found and fixed that sentence fragment. Thank you for pointing it out.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like this quite a bit, actually. Neat concept, and I like the interview for the same reasons as the Salmander.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like how frank the interview is. Most interview logs you see on this site either have class-Ds freaking out or some kind of secretive resistance. This dude just had a hallucination and then talked about it. It's oddly refreshing. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's a prolonged agitation; several hours. I'll see about adding a length of time to that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yaaaaay!!!!!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: and i like both. i am the missing link!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I dislike the hallucinations, but I think the pulling another world here with their music bit is nice. So I'm kind of the opposite of the guy above me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Doesn't emergence of strange flora when the birds are agitated contraindicate "standard traps"? Seems like you'd be better off gassing them, whether lethally or non-lethally.

Also "Addendum 01: It appears that when instances of SCP-1028 are in distress for a prolonged period of time, an emergence of one or more sprouts of unusually-colored flora in the vicinity of the distressed animal." seems to be missing a verb regarding the flora <doing something> in the vicinity.

Personally, I think the addition of the flora and alien worlds is unnecessary. Flocks of birds (angry birds!) that cause hallucinations are just fine as they are. Downvoted for now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Disregarding former edits of this comment, edits of this article have definitely improved it. +1
